# Idiopathic inflammatory myopathies related lung disease in adults

Sameep Sehgal*, Aditi Patel*, Soumya Chatterjee, Anthony P Fernandez, Carol Farver, Ruchi Yadav, Yuebing Li, Sonye K Danoff, Didem Saygin, Julio A Huapaya, Erin M Wilfong, Kristin B Highland

Lancet Respir Med Interstitial lung disease (ILD) is common in idiopathic inflammatory myopathies in adults, especially in patients with antisynthetase syndrome and anti-MDA5 antibody-associated dermatomyositis. Pulmonary manifestations can range from subclinical ILD to rapidly progressive respiratory failure. Coexistent myositis, characteristic skin lesions, arthritis, and Raynaud’s phenomenon are common. However, 16–65% of patients present with isolated lung disease. Detection of myositis-specific and myositis-associated antibodies can aid in diagnosis and disease characterisation. Chest imaging and pathology most commonly show non-specific interstitial pneumonia and organising pneumonia patterns. Immunosuppression is the mainstay of management with aggressive combination treatment for rapidly progressive disease and incremental escalation as needed for chronic ILD. The addition of antifibrotic agents is an option in progressive fibrotic disease, and lung transplantation can be considered in severe, end-stage disease. Most patients respond to treatment, but short-term mortality remains high for patients with rapidly progressive disease associated with anti-MDA5 antibody ILD.

# Introduction

Idiopathic inflammatory myopathies are a heterogeneous group of diseases primarily characterised by the common feature of immune-mediated inflammation of muscle fibres and surrounding tissues. The annual incidence of idiopathic inflammatory myopathies ranges from 0·2–2·0 per 100000 person-years, with a prevalence ranging from 2–25 per 100000 people.¹ However, these systemic diseases can manifest with a combination of inflammatory muscle, skin, lung, and joint disease. With clinical features, serology, and muscle pathology, currently six main phenotypes have been described in adults. Each phenotype has a distinct typical presentation (table 1).

# Key messages

- Interstitial lung disease (ILD) is common in patients with idiopathic inflammatory myopathies, especially those with antisynthetase syndrome and anti-MDA5 antibody.
- The clinical presentation of idiopathic inflammatory myopathy-associated ILD can vary from mild asymptomatic disease to acute respiratory failure. Nonspecific interstitial pneumonia and organising pneumonia are the most common patterns observed on imaging and pathology.
- Extrapulmonary manifestations of myositis, skin rash, arthritis, and Raynaud’s phenomenon are common. Lung disease might present in isolation or precede extrapulmonary disease. The presence of myositis-specific and myositis-associated antibodies can help to establish the diagnosis and characterise the clinical disease.
- Immunosuppression is the mainstay of treatment for pulmonary and extrapulmonary manifestations of idiopathic inflammatory myopathies. Early, high-potency combination treatment is used with a step-down approach in rapidly progressive disease. Conversely, slow, incremental escalation of immunosuppression is preferred for chronic ILD in a step-up approach.
- Most patients improve or stabilise on treatment; however, mortality remains high for rapidly progressive disease. Lung transplantation is an option for severe, progressive disease in a subgroup of patients.

Idiopathic inflammatory myopathies can affect multiple compartments of the respiratory system including lung parenchyma (resulting in ILD), pulmonary vasculature (leading to pulmonary hypertension), and respiratory muscles. Aspiration pneumonitis or pneumonia from dysphagia is also seen. ILD is the most common pulmonary manifestation, with an overall estimated prevalence of 40–50%. protein 5 (MDA5)-dermatomyositis and antisynthetase syndrome, the prevalence is more than 80%. The prevalence of anti-MDA5-dermatomyositis is higher in Asian cohorts than European cohorts. ILD in idiopathic inflammatory myopathies can be progressive and carries high morbidity and mortality. Treatment refractory and rapidly progressive disease has been associated most with anti-MDA5-dermatomyositis. This Review will discuss pulmonary and extrapulmonary clinical manifestations, diagnostic evaluation, treatment options, management approach, and future directions in adult patients with idiopathic inflammatory myopathy-associated ILD (IIM-ILD). We focus on antisynthetase syndrome and dermatomyositis; however, the principles can apply to any idiopathic inflammatory myopathy lung disease phenotype.

Several HLA and non-HLA genetic associations have been shown in idiopathic inflammatory myopathies. Epidemiological studies have reported smoking as a risk factor for antisynthetase syndrome. Preceding respiratory and gastrointestinal infections are associated with idiopathic inflammatory myopathy onset, specifically Coxsackievirus for dermatomyositis and anti-MDA5 dermatomyositis. ILD in idiopathic inflammatory myopathies can be progressive and carries high morbidity and mortality. This Review will discuss pulmonary and extrapulmonary clinical manifestations, diagnostic evaluation, treatment options, management approach, and future directions in adult patients with idiopathic inflammatory myopathy-associated ILD (IIM-ILD). Figure 1: Proposed pathobiology of myositis-associated ILD (A) Immune activators and environmental factors can trigger aberrant immune responses in a genetically susceptible individual. (B) Anti-MDA5 dermatomyositis is caused by infected cells that activate MDA5, which triggers type-1 IFN production. Virus induced cell injury and lysis, causes release of viral-MDA5 complexes that are recognised by antigen-presenting cells (macrophages) with subsequent activation of helper T cells (CD8) and B cells (CD4). As a result, there is production of anti-MDA5 autoantibodies and release of interleukins, TNF-α, and other inflammatory cytokines. (C) Antisynthetase syndrome is caused by cellular stress from epithelial injury that leads to cell death with microparticle release and danger signal pathway activation. APCs bind to TCRs on CD8⁺T cells. Along with NK cell activation, proteolytic enzymes (granzyme B) are produced, which leads to HisRS generation. The release of HisRS antigen in the extracellular milieu leads to inflammatory response activation, cytokine release, and production of HisRS autoantibodies. (D) Immune activation can lead to multisystem inflammation and fibrosis in the lungs, muscle, and skin. APC=antigen-presenting cell. DC=dendritic cell. HisRS=histidyl-tRNA-synthetase. IFN=interferon. ILD=interstitial lung disease. MDA5=melanoma differentiation-associated protein 5. NETosis=neutrophil extracellular traps and cell death. NK=natural killer. TCR=T-cell receptor. TNF-α=tumour necrosis factor-α.

immune checkpoint inhibitors, d-penicillamine, interferons, and anti-TNF agents are also potential triggers. A seasonal distribution with higher prevalence of anti-MDA5 dermatomyositis in colder months could be related to infections. A higher prevalence of antisynthetase syndrome and dermatomyositis has been noted with a latitudinal gradient, suggesting an association with increased intensity of ultraviolet radiation. Antisynthetase syndrome is characterised by the development of autoantibodies directed against different tRNA-synthetase enzymes. Activation of adaptive immune cells and tolerance breakdown leads to antigen presentation, CD8⁺ T cell priming, and CD4-T cell–B cell crosstalk. Release of type I and type II interferons, B lymphocyte stimulator, and other cytokines lead to the production of anti-tRNA synthetase autoantibodies. The resultant aberrant immune activation of T cells (including CD8 + and CD4 + T cells) is thought to play a central role in disease pathogenesis. Activated CD8⁺ T cells release granzyme B, which cleaves several aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases in the lungs. Active T cells, B cells, and autoantibodies enter the systemic circulation and encounter the altered autoantigens in the lungs, resulting in lung injury. Most of the 20 known aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases could become autoantigenic; however, commercially available tests only measure antibodies against some of them. The most common antibodies are anti-histidyl-tRNA-synthetase antibody or anti-Jo-1 antibody.

Clinical presentation and diagnostic testing The classical presentation of idiopathic inflammatory myopathy includes a combination of muscle, skin, lung, and joint disease. However, patients can initially manifest symptoms affecting any of these systems in isolation. The disease can become progressive and affect other organs after initial presentation. A multidisciplinary collaboration between pulmonology, rheumatology, dermatology, and neurology plays an important role in the diagnosis and management of these patients. Knowledge of the subtle signs of systemic manifestations of idiopathic inflammatory myopathy by specialists can help in early and accurate diagnosis.

Activated CD8⁺ T cells release granzyme B, which cleaves several aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases in the lungs. Active T cells, B cells, and autoantibodies enter the systemic circulation and encounter the altered autoantigens in the lungs, resulting in lung injury. Most of the 20 known aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases could become autoantigenic; however, commercially available tests only measure antibodies against some of them. The most common antibodies are anti-histidyl-tRNA-synthetase antibody or anti-Jo-1 antibody.

items.md: Clinical presentation and diagnostic testing The classical presentation of idiopathic inflammatory myopathy includes a combination of muscle, skin, lung, and joint disease. However, patients can initially manifest symptoms affecting any of these systems in isolation. The disease can become progressive and affect other organs after initial presentation. A multidisciplinary collaboration between pulmonology, rheumatology, dermatology, and neurology plays an important role in the diagnosis and management of these patients. Knowledge of the subtle signs of systemic manifestations of idiopathic inflammatory myopathy by specialists can help in early and accurate diagnosis.

pages.md:
# Review

# Serology

Autoantibodies in idiopathic inflammatory myopathy are closely linked to clinical phenotypes. In patients with suspected IIM-ILD, antibody testing can help establish a diagnosis, predict outcomes, and play a role in determining treatments. The group of antibodies specific for idiopathic inflammatory myopathy manifestations are known as myositis-specific antibodies and those frequently found in other connective tissue diseases, which can also cause myositis, are grouped into myositis-associated antibodies. Myositis-specific antibodies include antibodies for dermatomyositis (ie, anti-MDA5, Mi-2, TIF-1γ, NXP-2, and SAE), antisynthetase syndrome (ie, anti-Jo-1, EJ, OJ, PL-7, PL-12, Ha, Zo, and Ks), immune-mediated necrotising myopathy (ie, anti-HMGCR and anti-SRP), and inclusion body myositis (ie, anti-cN1A). Myositis-associated antibodies include anti-PM/Scl, anti-U1RNP, anti-U3RNP, anti-Ku, anti-cN1A, and anti-Ro52. Details of common clinical manifestations associated with the various myositis-specific antibodies are reviewed in table 1.

Anti-nuclear antibody testing is often used to screen for connective tissue disease. Indirect immunofluorescence using human epithelial type 2 cells as a substrate, is the standard screening for anti-nuclear antibodies with quantification of the titre and anti-nuclear antibody staining pattern. Most myositis-specific antibodies show positive cytoplasmic staining and a negative nuclear staining pattern, which can lead to a negative anti-nuclear antibody test. Therefore, anti-nuclear antibody is not useful for screening for the presence of a myositis-specific antibody. Several single and multiplex commercial assays are available for detecting most myositis-specific antibodies. These assays include ELISA, addressable laser bead immunoassays, immunoblotting techniques, and RNA or protein radio-immunoprecipitation. ELISA are the most used assay worldwide. Currently, the gold standard for myositis autoantibody testing is the radio-immunoprecipitation assay, which is not commonly available. In a multinational study evaluating real world antisynthetase antibody measurement, the local or commercial measurement of anti-Jo1 antibody was reliable compared with the gold standard RNA radio-immunoprecipitation measurement. The measurement of non-Jo1 antisynthetase antibodies (anti OJ, EJ, PL-12, and PL-7) were less reliable. Thus, the clinician should be aware of the limitations of the test being used and be mindful of the possibility of false-positive or false-negative results. Based on our experience, we suggest starting with a panel that includes all eight available antisynthetase antibodies, anti-MDA5, and anti-Ro52, which are antibodies that have been associated with ILDs. Other antibodies, such as anti-HMGCR, anti-SRP, or anti-CN1A can be ordered if clinically indicated.

# Pulmonary manifestations

# Interstitial lung disease

ILD is the most common pulmonary manifestation of idiopathic inflammatory myopathy, affecting over 80% of patients with antisynthetase syndrome and anti-MDA5 dermatomyositis. Other idiopathic inflammatory myopathy subtypes often present with muscle disease and have a much lower prevalence of and milder ILD. Lung disease preceding extrapulmonary symptoms is seen in 16–65% of patients, often by several months. Three distinct clinical presentations of IIM-ILD are frequently encountered. First, rapidly progressive ILD can be seen in an acute exacerbation of previously undiagnosed ILD or in patients with considerable worsening of ILD within 1–3 months of diagnosis. Acute hypoxic respiratory failure is commonly associated with anti-MDA5-dermatomyositis and antisynthetase syndrome, particularly with anti-Jo1, anti-PL7, and anti-PL12 antibodies. Development of spontaneous pneumomediastinum is a poor prognostic sign. Second, chronic ILD manifests as insidious onset of dyspnoea and cough, which can be associated with extrapulmonary symptoms of idiopathic inflammatory myopathy. Chronic ILD is the most common presentation in patients with antisynthetase syndrome. Lastly,

sub-clinical ILD can be seen on imaging with minimal or predominant reticulation and ground-glass opacities with no symptoms, particularly in patients with anti-SAE, anti-SRP, and anti-TIF1γ antibodies, which are associated with severe myositis. Occasionally, patients with antisynthetase syndrome might be in this category when extrapulmonary symptoms are predominant. Chest imaging is important for establishing a diagnosis, choosing therapy, and estimating the prognosis of IIM-ILD (figure 2). A high-resolution CT scan (<1·5 mm slices) with volumetric scanning of the chest with inspiratory and expiratory images can help characterise parenchymal disease. Prone images can assist with subtle, sub-pleural changes and distinguish from compression atelectasis or dependent oedema. Non-specific interstitial pneumonia is the most common pattern in chronic ILD and is seen in more than 50% of patients with IIM-ILD and is characterised by peripheral and lower lobe consolidation and ground-glass opacities. The presence of an organising pneumonia pattern on imaging is associated with a good response to immunosuppression and better outcomes. Concomitant presence of both non-specific interstitial pneumonia and organising pneumonia patterns are often observed in IIM-ILD. A usual intestinal pneumonia pattern is characterised by sub-pleural, predominantly basilar fibrosis, honeycombing, and the absence of ground-glass opacities. Usual interstitial pneumonia has been reported in 10–30% of patients with antisynthetase syndrome, usually in advanced disease. A usual interstitial pneumonia pattern is rare in anti-MDA5 dermatomyositis and is seen in less than 1% of patients. Additional radiographic features have been associated with usual interstitial pneumonia due to connective tissue disease and include the concentration of fibrosis in the anterior upper lobes, exuberant honeycomb-like cysts in the fibrotic parenchyma, and lower lobe predominant fibrosis with sharp demarcation with normal lung in the craniocaudal plane. The diffuse alveolar damage pattern consists of diffuse ground-glass opacities and consolidation, and is commonly seen during an acute exacerbation of chronic ILD or in rapidly progressive ILD, particularly in patients with anti-MDA5 dermatomyositis. Usual interstitial pneumonia and diffuse alveolar damage patterns are associated with poor response to treatment and worse outcomes. The imaging patterns can overlap, evolve, and progress. PET scans can show an increase in fluorodeoxy glucose uptake in both fibrotic and inflammatory ILD.

(H) Patient with antisynthetase syndrome (anti-Jo-1 antibody). Coronal CT image shows a usual interstitial pneumonia pattern (subpleural and lower lobe predominant coarse reticulation with architectural distortion, traction bronchiectasis, traction bronchiolectasis, and honeycombing). Coronal CT shows upper lobe predominant bullous emphysema with lower lobe predominant usual interstitial pneumonia pattern (subpleural reticulation, traction bronchiectasis, traction bronchiolectasis, and honeycombing). A usual interstitial pneumonia pattern is rare in anti-MDA5 dermatomyositis and is seen in less than 1% of patients. Additional radiographic features have been associated with usual interstitial pneumonia due to connective tissue disease and include the concentration of fibrosis in the anterior upper lobes, exuberant honeycomb-like cysts in the fibrotic parenchyma, and lower lobe predominant fibrosis with sharp demarcation with normal lung in the craniocaudal plane. The diffuse alveolar damage pattern consists of diffuse ground-glass opacities and consolidation, and is commonly seen during an acute exacerbation of chronic ILD or in rapidly progressive ILD, particularly in patients with anti-MDA5 dermatomyositis. Usual interstitial pneumonia and diffuse alveolar damage patterns are associated with poor response to treatment and worse outcomes. The imaging patterns can overlap, evolve, and progress. PET scans can show an increase in fluorodeoxy glucose uptake in both fibrotic and inflammatory ILD.

In patients with IIM-ILD, an increased fluorodeoxy glucose uptake (>2·5 standardised uptake value) is more prevalent in anti-MDA5 ILD than in patients who are negative for anti-MDA5, and has been associated with worse outcomes. 40,41 PET scans are not used in routine practice, but could potentially have a role in prognostication.42

A restrictive pattern (ie, low total lung capacity and FVC) along with a low diffusion of carbon monoxide (DLCO) is the most common finding on pulmonary function tests (PFTs). Patients with mild disease can have an isolated mild reduction in DLCO with normal FVC. Concomitant ILD and emphysema or pulmonary hypertension might lead to a reduction in DLCO, which is out of proportion to the reduction in FVC. In patients with respiratory muscle weakness, PFTs usually reveal a decreased FVC, normal DLCO, a greater than 20% drop in FVC when laying supine, decrease in maximum voluntary ventilation, and decreased maximal inspiratory and expiratory pressures.

Lung biopsy is rarely needed to establish a diagnosis of IIM-ILD with classical clinical, radiographic, and serological presentations (appendix p 1). Occasionally, lung biopsy might be needed to rule out malignancy. Non-specific interstitial pneumonia is the most common pathological finding.43 A usual interstitial pneumonia pattern is seen in a few patients. Both non-specific interstitial pneumonia and usual interstitial pneumonia might be associated with an acute pathology, either diffuse alveolar damage, or organising pneumonia.

# Pulmonary hypertension

Pulmonary hypertension has been reported in 8–10% of patients with antisynthetase syndrome with most patients (81%) having severe disease at diagnosis. Pulmonary hypertension is most commonly associated with concomitant advanced ILD (World Symposium for Pulmonary Hypertension [WSPH] group 3 disease). 30 However, pulmonary arterial hypertension (WSPH group 1) in the absence of ILD or out of proportion to the degree of ILD has also been reported in antisynthetase syndrome and other idiopathic inflammatory myopathies.44 Pulmonary hypertension in the absence of ILD is rare in anti-MDA5 dermatomyositis and data are limited to case reports.45 Occasionally, pulmonary hypertension can be a consequence of myocarditis, inflammatory infiltration in the cardiac conduction system, and replacement fibrosis (WSPH group 2). Patients can present with angina, dysrhythmias, or congestive heart failure. Cardiac MRI is a sensitive technique that can detect mild inflammation in the myocardium.46,47 Echocardiogram is used as a screening test to establish if further investigations for pulmonary hypertension are warranted, but the clinician needs to maintain a high clinical suspicion as echocardiography in patients with parenchymal lung disease can be inaccurate. The diagnosis of pulmonary hypertension requires confirmation by right heart catheterisation.

# Constitutional symptoms

Fever is the presenting symptom in 25% of patients with antisynthetase syndrome and is seen in 40% of patients.

pages.md:
# Review

during their disease course. Antisynthetase syndrome should be on the diﬀerential diagnosis for fever of unknown origin. Weight loss can be associated with disease activity, although other important causes, such as an underlying neoplasm, should also be considered.

# Musculoskeletal manifestations

Myopathy is a hallmark feature of idiopathic inflammatory myopathy (appendix p 2). The characteristic presentation is symmetric—proximal muscle weakness with hip and thigh muscles being the most frequently aﬀected areas. Common symptoms are diﬃculty standing from a seated position, climbing steps, or raising arms overhead. Neck extensor muscle weakness can cause head drop and pharyngeal muscle weakness might result in dysphagia and voice change. Myalgia and muscle tenderness are common with antisynthetase syndrome. A detailed neurological examination is crucial in determining the severity and distribution of muscle weakness and to rule out myositis mimics.

In patients with antisynthetase syndrome, lung disease in the absence of or preceding myositis by months is typical. The absence of myopathy is most often seen in anti-MDA5 dermatomyositis, but can also be associated with other myositis-specific antibodies. Clinically, patients with amyopathic dermatomyositis are at higher risk of developing ILD than patients with classic dermatomyositis, and clinically amyopathic dermatomyositis is considered a predictor of rapidly progressive ILD.

The diagnostic investigations for myopathy include a variable combination of muscle enzymes, muscle MRI, electromyography, and muscle biopsy. Creatine kinase is the most sensitive and measurable muscle enzyme and can be used to monitor treatment responses and relapses. Other non-specific biomarkers of muscle injury include aldolase, lactate dehydrogenase, and aminotransferases.

Bilateral thigh MRI using a myositis protocol (T1-weighted imaging, fluid-sensitive T2W with fat suppression, or short-tau inversion recovery sequence) can be used to identify early stages of myositis and target areas for a muscle biopsy. Findings include oedema of muscle fibres, alteration in muscle signal intensity, and, in later stages, presence of fatty infiltration, fibrosis, or muscle atrophy. These changes are not specific to myositis and can also be seen in injuries, muscle infarction, denervation, or rhabdomyolysis.

Electromyography can distinguish myopathy from neuropathic causes of weakness. Myopathy is characterised by polyphasic motor unit action potentials that are of short-duration, low amplitude on voluntary activation, and with prominent muscle membrane irritability at rest. Electromyography is not diagnostic of myositis, as similar findings can occur in infectious, toxic, metabolic, or degenerative myopathies. Electromyography can guide muscle biopsy, but biopsy should not be performed on a muscle that has recently undergone electromyography testing.

Nailfold capillaroscopy can aid in diagnosis and prognostication. Findings include enlarged capillaries.

Electromyography is not diagnostic of myositis, as similar findings can occur in infectious, toxic, metabolic, or degenerative myopathies. Electromyography can guide muscle biopsy, but biopsy should not be performed on a muscle that has recently undergone electromyography testing.

Adult onset idiopathic inflammatory myopathy is associated with an increased risk of malignancy, particularly within the 3 years before and 3 years after onset. Furthermore, cancer is a leading cause of death in adults with idiopathic inflammatory myopathy. Various cancers including lung, ovarian, colorectal, breast, lymphoma, and nasopharyngeal are among the most.

# Table 2: Evolution of classification criteria for immune-mediated myositis

Clinical features, and antibody status. Enhanced cancer screening is recommended in patients who are at intermediate or high risk, which includes CT scans, pelvic ultrasound, mammography, cervical cancer screening, and cancer biomarkers. A whole-body PET scan should be considered in patients at high risk where other investigations have not detected a malignancy.⁷³

Classification criteria for idiopathic inflammatory myopathy

Bohan and Peter provided classification criteria for dermatomyositis in 1975, which was widely used for 40 years (table 2).74,75 They included characteristic dermatomyositis rashes and the need to exclude other causes of myopathy, but did not mention pulmonary manifestations. Patients with anti-Jo-1 antibody were recognised as having a distinct phenotype termed antisynthetase syndrome in the early 1990s.79,80 Since then, idiopathic inflammatory myopathies have been further classified based on clinical manifestations and antibody profiles. In 2017, the first validated classification criteria of idiopathic inflammatory myopathy by the American College of Rheumatology and European Alliance of Associations for Rheumatology (EULAR) were developed and included the probability of having idiopathic inflammatory myopathy. 78 The criteria include age of onset, pattern of muscle weakness, muscle enzyme elevation, muscle biopsy findings, presence of rash, and the presence of anti-Jo-1 antibody. The criteria do not include ILD or non-Jo-1 antibodies. The criteria also do not define antisynthetase syndrome as a distinct idiopathic inflammatory myopathy subtype.

Management

Immunosuppression is the mainstay of treatment for IIM-ILD targeting several pathways. As with most rare diseases, few clinical trials exist, and treatment varies based on clinical presentation and centre preference. Patients with rapidly progressive ILD need early, aggressive, combination anti-inflammatory therapy. Patients with chronic ILD can be treated with a step-up escalation of therapy over several months. Patients with subclinical disease can be monitored without therapy but require close follow-up. Treatment decisions are based on expert opinion, experience, the clinical gestalt of individual treatment teams and require multidisciplinary collaboration. A summary of our recommendations on initial dosing, common adverse events, and monitoring of immunomodulators commonly used in patients with IIM-ILD are detailed in the appendix (pp 3–6).

pages.md:
# Review

# Corticosteroids

Corticosteroids have been the first-line treatment for pulmonary and extrapulmonary manifestations of idiopathic inflammatory myopathy for decades. A meta-analysis of patients with IIM-ILD (45% antisynthetase syndrome and 40% dermatomyositis), reviewed the effect of various treatment strategies. Six studies in the analysis included 124 patients who received corticosteroid mono-therapy. Clinical improvement was reported in 112 (89%) of the 124 patients. However, the meta-analysis had several limitations and none of the included studies were controlled. Most studies evaluating other therapies for IIM-ILD have patients on background corticosteroids, thus limiting the data available on using a steroid-free approach.

In clinical practice, most patients are started on corticosteroids at the time of diagnosis either as a single agent or in combination with a steroid-sparing agent. In patients with rapidly progressive-ILD, high dose intravenous steroids (500–1000 mg methylprednisolone for 3 days) in combination with other immunomodulators are started. Advantages of using corticosteroids include quick onset, low cost, and physician experience. However, long-term use of corticosteroids carries considerable morbidity, including steroid myopathy. Given the morbidity associated with long-term corticosteroids, we advocate to start them in conjunction with a steroid-sparing agent early in the course of disease.

# Antimetabolites

Mycophenolate mofetil (MMF), methotrexate, and azathioprine block lymphocyte activation and proliferation by inhibiting DNA synthesis via various mechanisms. In patients with IIM-ILD, antimetabolites are commonly used as first-line steroid-sparing agents due to good patient tolerance and favourable adverse effect profiles. However, data are limited to retrospective studies. In a single-centre, retrospective study of 125 patients with connective tissue disease-associated ILD (32 [25.6%] with idiopathic inflammatory myopathy), MMF treatment led to improvement or stabilisation of disease across various underlying connective tissue diseases. Patients with usual interstitial pneumonia pathology showed stabilisation and those with non-usual interstitial pneumonia had improvement in lung function.

In a retrospective review of patients with IIM-ILD, both MMF and azathioprine led to improvement or stabilisation of lung function (FVC improvement of 3.6% in MMF group and 3.3% in azathioprine group) and a significant reduction in prednisone dose (6.9 mg in MMF group and 15 mg in azathioprine group) at 24 months. The rate of adverse effects was slightly higher in the azathioprine group.

# Rituximab

Rituximab is an anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody that causes peripheral B cell depletion. The RECITAL randomised controlled trial compared rituximab with cyclophosphamide as second-line agents in 101 patients with connective tissue disease-associated ILD (44 [43.6%] with IIM-ILD). Both rituximab and cyclophosphamide increased the median FVC at 24 weeks (94 mL rituximab and 99 mL cyclophosphamide) and 48 weeks (112 mL rituximab and 138 mL cyclophosphamide). DLCO, 6-min walk distance, and quality of life also improved in both groups. The cyclophosphamide group had more total adverse effects, but both had a similar number of serious adverse events. This randomised control trial showed that most patients treated with second line rituximab or cyclophosphamide had improvements in lung function.

# Cyclophosphamide

Cyclophosphamide is an alkylating agent, commonly used for cancer chemotherapy, which is used for induction in life-threatening autoimmune conditions due to its rapid and profound immune suppression. In addition to data from the RECITAL randomised controlled trial, similar findings were seen in a systematic review of five studies analysing cyclophosphamide use in patients with IIM-ILD (55% with anti-Jo-1 antibodies). In the review, 71% of patients had improvement in FVC, 69% had improvement in DLCO, and 67% had improvement in chest CT findings.⁸¹ Cyclophosphamide can be administered up to 6 months in severe or rapidly progressive ILD, with transition to less toxic agents once the disease is in remission.

# JAK inhibitors

Tofacitinib is a JAK inhibitor that preferentially inhibits JAK1 and JAK3. Tofacitinib suppresses the interferon pathway and various cytokines that are implicated in the pathogenesis of anti-MDA5-dermatomyositis. Thus, currently available data mainly focus on tofacitinib use in anti-MDA5 dermatomyositis. In a single-centre, open-label trial with retrospective controls, Chen and colleagues studied the eﬃcacy of tofacitinib as a first-line steroid-sparing agent in 18 patients with anti-MDA5 ILD, compared with 32 historical controls. 93 The 6-month survival was 100% in the tofacitinib group compared with 78% in controls. They reported an improvement in FVC, DLCO, and chest CT scores in the tofacitinib group. Fan and colleagues compared tofacitinib with tacrolimus in a retrospective study of patients with anti-MDA5 dermatomyositis. More than 50% of patients had rapidly progressive ILD. Lower mortality in the tofacitinib group was seen at 6 months (38·5% tofacitinib vs 62·9% tacrolimus) and 1 year (44·0% tofacitinib vs 65·7% tacrolimus).⁹⁴ Tofacitinib has also shown eﬃcacy in patients with disease refractory to other agents. 95,96 Tofacitinib is orally dosed and can be used as a second-line or third-line agent for anti-MDA5 dermatomyositis. The use of other JAK inhibitors such as baricitinib have been reported in a case series with clinical trials ongoing. 97,98

# Therapeutic plasma exchange

Therapeutic plasma exchange can decrease the levels of autoantibodies, cytokines, and immune complexes. Data for therapeutic plasma exchange in IIM-ILD is restricted to case series. In a retrospective study of 11 patients who underwent therapeutic plasma exchange for anti-MDA5 ILD, survival was ten (90%) in the therapeutic plasma exchange group compared with four (50%) of eight patients in the non-therapeutic plasma exchange group.¹⁰⁶ With scarce data, therapeutic plasma exchange continues to be used as a salvage therapy for rapidly progressive ILD.¹⁰⁷

pages.md:
# Lung transplantation

Lung transplantation is an option for selected patients with end-stage lung disease. Patients with progressive disease despite appropriate therapy, need for supplemental oxygen, progressive fibrotic disease, and rapidly progressive ILD should be considered for transplant evaluation.1⁰⁸ In addition to standard issues of candidate selection, extrapulmonary manifestations of idiopathic inflammatory myopathy can affect candidacy and post-transplant outcomes.109 Active myositis can affect rehabilitation potential, ventilator weaning, and the risk of aspiration. Therefore, myositis needs to be quiescent before transplant referral. Patients with considerable respiratory muscle weakness are not considered candidates for lung transplantation. Post-transplant outcomes in IIM-ILD are similar to those for other indications in US and European cohorts.110,111 Timely referral to an experienced transplant centre can help improve the likelihood of a successful transplant.

# Management of chronic ILD

# First-line immune modulators

| All patients:                                                        | Corticosteroids; mycophenolate or azathioprine                              |
| -------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Progression, lack of considerable improvement, or relapse of disease | Monitor for medication adverse effects; pneumocystis pneumonia prophylaxis† |

# Second-line immune modulators

Rituximab; tacrolimus or cyclosporine; intravenous immunoglobulin
Progression or relapse of disease

# Third-line immune modulators

| Anti-fibrotic for pulmonary function tests\*              | Cyclophosphamide                    |
| --------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| Nintedanib (after an adequate trial of immunosuppression) | Lung transplant evaluation          |
| Respiratory muscle weakness:                              | Need for supplemental oxygen        |
|                                                           | Non-invasive mechanical ventilation |

# Management of rapidly progressive ILD

# First-line combination therapy

| All patients:                                  | Corticosteroid pulse (500-1000 mg per day for 3 days)                                                 |
| ---------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| AND one or two agents depending on severity:   | tacrolimus or cyclosporine; cyclophosphamide (maximum 6 months); rituximab; tofacitinib; IVIg         |
| Progression or lack of significant improvement | Monitor for medication adverse effects; pulmonary rehabilitation; pneumocystis pneumonia prophylaxis† |

# Salvage

| Therapeutic plasma exchange | IVIg                                                            |
| --------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Lung transplant evaluation  | (if isolated respiratory failure)                               |
| Evaluate ECMO candidacy     | (bridge to recovery or transplant in acute respiratory failure) |

# Infection prevention and prophylaxis

Patients on immunosuppression treatment are at increased risk for infectious complications. Prophylaxis for pneumocystis pneumonia with trimethoprim–sulfamethoxazole or an alternate regimen should be started in patients on two immunosuppression medications or those on high-dose corticosteroids (>20 mg per day prednisone for 1 month). Age-specific and location-specific immunisations before initiating immunosuppression treatment and seasonal annual immunisation is strongly recommended by the authors.

# Management of pulmonary hypertension

In patients with WSPH group 1 pulmonary arterial hypertension, treatment according to the latest WSPH guidelines for group 1 disease should be pursued. Immunosuppression has been reported to be beneficial in observational studies in patients with systematic lupus erythematosus and mixed connective tissue disease-associated pulmonary hypertension.113 However, there is no supporting data in patients with idiopathic inflammatory myopathy-related pulmonary hypertension.

In patients with WSPH group 3 pulmonary hypertension due to ILD, inhaled treprostinil is recommended as it is shown to increase 6-min walk distance (mean increase 31 m), improve biomarkers of right heart failure (nt-pro BNP), and decrease the incidence of clinical deterioration (22% treprostinil vs 33% control).¹¹⁴

# Management of respiratory muscle weakness

Aggressive immune modulation with high-dose steroids, IVIg, and other agents should be initiated depending on the degree of muscle weakness. Patients need to be monitored closely for worsening disease with measurements of negative inspiratory force and vital capacity. A negative inspiratory force of less than 30 cmH2O and FVC of less than 20 mL per kg might warrant monitoring in an intensive care unit setting. Patients might progress to respiratory failure and could require non-invasive or invasive mechanical ventilation. Management in this situation is similar to other neuromuscular diseases affecting the respiratory system.115 Non-invasive ventilation can help improve hypercapnia, sleep quality, and quality of life in patients with chronic hypoventilation.

pages.md:
# Review

# Monitoring

The goal of ongoing monitoring is to assess response to therapy, progression of disease, medication toxicity, infections, and comorbidities. The frequency is based on the severity, rate of disease progression, and comorbidities, and is altered to individual patient needs. In patients with chronic ILD, patients should be evaluated 1–3 months after initiating or altering therapy. Patients with rapidly progressive ILD and anti-MDA5 dermatomyositis are often admitted to hospital for rapid initiation of combination therapy. Post-hospital discharge visits are often every 2–4 weeks initially. Repeat imaging can help in assessing response to therapy by evaluating the inflammatory findings from imaging. Once the disease is deemed quiescent, patients with stable disease should be evaluated every 3–6 months.

In addition to assessing symptoms and medication toxicity, PFTs are checked (spirometry and DLCO) every 3–6 months and a chest CT scan performed as needed. At any time, worsening symptoms or hospitalisation should trigger a detailed re-evaluation, which includes investigations for infection or pulmonary hypertension. Disease can be considered progressive if there are worsening symptoms, a drop in FVC (>5%), a drop in DLCO (>10%), worsening oxygenation, or worsening parenchymal disease on imaging. Progressive disease is common and is reported in 18% of patients in the first year.

# Treatment approach

Our management approach is based on our experience, disease acuity, severity, and comorbidities (figure 4). In patients with incidental, mild, or subclinical disease with no symptoms and normal pulmonary physiology, a wait and watch approach can be adopted. Patients should be closely monitored for the development of symptoms and PFT should be measured every 3–6 months for any signs of progression.

In patients with chronic ILD, a step-up approach should be used starting with a single agent, monitoring response, and stepping up treatment over weeks or months. First-line therapy includes moderate dose corticosteroids (prednisone 0·5–1·0 mg per kg), with tapering over 2–3 months to the lowest tolerated dose. Depending on severity, an antimetabolite is often started within weeks. We use rituximab or calcineurin inhibitors as second-line agents either alone or in combination with IVIg. Cyclophosphamide can be used as a third-line agent in refractory disease. Antifibrotic medications are added in progressive pulmonary fibrosis if there is progression on optimal anti-inflammatory therapies.

# Prognosis

The estimated 1-year survival in patients with IIM-ILD is greater than 90% and 10-year cumulative survival is around 80%. When compared with patients with IPF, survival is considerably better in IIM-ILD. Patients with anti-MDA5 dermatomyositis continue to have a high short-term mortality, with 75% having a 1-year survival. Acute exacerbations of ILD carry a high short-term mortality irrespective of the underlying idiopathic inflammatory myopathy. The overall age-adjusted mortality rate for IIM-ILD in the USA has been stable for the past two decades. Factors associated with lower survival are anti-MDA5 and anti PL-7 antibody positivity, older age at onset, rapidly progressive ILD, poor response to therapy, elevated inflammatory markers, usual interstitial pneumonia pattern, malignancy, and concomitant pulmonary hypertension.

# Search strategy and selection criteria

We searched MEDLINE and PubMed for original articles about idiopathic inflammatory myopathy-associated interstitial lung disease published from Jan 1, 1980, to March 1, 2024. Both human and animal studies were considered. We used the terms “polymyositis” OR “dermatomyositis” OR “antisynthetase” AND “interstitial lung disease”. We screened the reference lists of the articles identified for studies that fulfilled the search criteria that were not identified during the initial search. No measures were used to exclude any studies in the literature search for this Review. Only English language papers were considered.

# Conclusions

Idiopathic inflammatory myopathies are a common cause of chronic and rapidly progressive ILD, often with isolated lung disease. Recognition of idiopathic inflammatory myopathy as a cause of ILD is essential since most patients respond to initial immunosuppression treatment. The widespread availability of myositis-associated antibodies and testing can help improve diagnosis and phenotyping of this disease. Notably, there is an absence.

Patients with anti-MDA5 dermatomyositis continue to have a high short-term mortality, with 75% having a 1-year survival. Acute exacerbations of ILD carry a high short-term mortality irrespective of the underlying idiopathic inflammatory myopathy. The overall age-adjusted mortality rate for IIM-ILD in the USA has been stable for the past two decades. Factors associated with lower survival are anti-MDA5 and anti PL-7 antibody positivity, older age at onset, rapidly progressive ILD, poor response to therapy, elevated inflammatory markers, usual interstitial pneumonia pattern, malignancy, and concomitant pulmonary hypertension.

items.md: # Search strategy and selection criteria
items.value: Search strategy and selection criteria

items.md: We searched MEDLINE and PubMed for original articles about idiopathic inflammatory myopathy-associated interstitial lung disease published from Jan 1, 1980, to March 1, 2024. Both human and animal studies were considered. We used the terms “polymyositis” OR “dermatomyositis” OR “antisynthetase” AND “interstitial lung disease”. We screened the reference lists of the articles identified for studies that fulfilled the search criteria that were not identified during the initial search. No measures were used to exclude any studies in the literature search for this Review. Only English language papers were considered.

items.md: # Conclusions
items.value: Conclusions

items.md: Idiopathic inflammatory myopathies are a common cause of chronic and rapidly progressive ILD, often with isolated lung disease. Recognition of idiopathic inflammatory myopathy as a cause of ILD is essential since most patients respond to initial immunosuppression treatment. # Review

of reliable biomarkers to guide therapy and monitor the clinical response. Furthermore, better diagnostic criteria for idiopathic inflammatory myopathy should recognise isolated IIM-ILD (ie, pneumomyositis) and antisynthetase syndrome.

Several treatments continue to be investigated in patients with idiopathic inflammatory myopathy, with many ongoing clinical trials. Immunomodulators targeting various pathways are being repurposed for use in IIM-ILD. Case reports have shown the benefits of daratumumab (anti-CD38 antibody), basiliximab (IL-2 receptor antagonist), tocilizumab (IL-6 antagonist), abatacept (selective co-stimulation modulator), anakinra (IL-1 receptor antagonist), and ruxolitinib (JAK inhibitor) among others. The infusion of chimeric antigen receptor T cells that recognise CD19⁺ B cells can lead to their depletion and an “immune reset”. This therapy has shown promise in inducing remission in patients with refractory systemic lupus erythematosus. Two recent case reports showed remission in patients with refractory antisynthetase syndrome with chimeric antigen receptor T cell therapy. Although these findings are encouraging, data are scarce, and this study is still considered investigational.
